question.text: Category I and II AIFs must be:

answer.text: Close-ended with a minimum tenure of three years
Either open or close-ended at manager's discretion
Close-ended with a maximum tenure of one year

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Category I and Category II AIFs shall be close-ended and the tenure of each scheme shall be determined at time of SEBI application, subject to a minimum tenure of three years. This ensures long-term investment commitment aligned with alternative asset illiquidity. **The Trap:** Only Category III AIFs can be open-ended. The 3-year minimum applies to scheme tenure, not the trust/entity tenure. **Cross-Exam Context:** The close-ended nature of Cat I and II AIFs distinguishes them from mutual funds (open-ended) and is fundamental to the AIF framework. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The minimum 3-year tenure means investors' capital is locked in. In practice, most funds have 7-10 year tenures. Ensure investors understand there's no early exit route like mutual fund redemption.
